title: Why Most Real Estate Agents Quit in Their First Year
dek: The real estate industry has a quiet statistic it doesn't lead with: the majority of new agents are gone within their first couple of years. Not because real estate doesn't work. Because the way new agents are told to do it doesn't work for people who need income.
heroAlt: Why Most Real Estate Agents Quit in Their First Year

blocks.text: The real killer is the gap

blocks.html: Here's the anatomy of a typical first-year failure. An agent gets licensed, joins a brokerage, and follows the standard advice: focus on sales, build your sphere, be patient. Meanwhile, the meter is running: MLS dues, association fees, marketing, gas, and the small matter of rent and groceries.

Their first closing is months away on the best timeline. Their expenses are due monthly. That mismatch, income on a six-month clock and bills on a thirty-day clock, is the gap. And the gap, not the market and not their talent, is what removes most agents from the business.

blocks.text: They didn't lose the game. They ran out of clock.
Talent isn't the filter. Cash flow is.

blocks.html: The uncomfortable truth is that the sales-first path functions as a wealth filter. Agents who arrive with a year of savings, a working spouse, or a fat sphere of influence can wait out the gap. Everyone else, including plenty of people with real ability, gets filtered out while they're still learning.
That's why "just be patient" is such hollow advice. Patience is a luxury good. New agents need a source of income that matches the speed of their obligations.

blocks.text: The fix is structural, not motivational

blocks.html: You can't affirmation your way across a six-month income gap. You close it structurally, with transactions that pay on a short cycle. In residential real estate, that means rentals: deals that close in days, pay roughly a month's rent per lease, and stack month after month while your longer-cycle business grows.
The agents who survive year one aren't tougher. They're better structured. They built a cash flow engine first and a career on top of it.

blocks.text: Your first year should end with a business, not a story about one.

blocks.html: If you're a new agent staring at that gap right now, my free ebook Luxury Leases, Real Profits shows you exactly how to close it. Better agents win.
